coredata.dc:title: Influence of silver nitrate in obtaining highly luminescent sodium-doped carbon dots for white light emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jiaxin
Zeng, Qiwen
Song, Jie
Tie, Shaolong
Lin, Zhenxu
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent metal-doping carbon dots (CDs) possess excellent optical properties for light-converting phosphors of white light emitting diode (LED). However, the photoluminescence quantum yields (PL QYs) of CDs still suffer from the trapping defect at the surface of CDs which may generate the detrimental nonradiative recombination centers. Herein, we developed an N doping method to enhance blue light emission of Na+-doped CDs via a modified solid-state reaction, using silver nitrate as nitrogen source. The blue PL intensity form Na+-doped CDs is improved by the increasing molar ratio of silver nitrate precursor. The obtained Na+ and N co-doping CDs exhibited 5-fold enhancement of PL intensity with a PL QYs up to 24% as compared to their undoped counterpart. Based on the temporal evolution of PL and surface structure characteristics, it suggested that the improvement of PL QYs derived from the elimination of the trapping defects at the surface of Na+-doped CDs. This is realized by the introduction of nitrate groups and Ag+ ions, which can reduce the formation of hydroxyl radicals related nonradiative recombination centers. Furthermore, the Na+ and N co-doped CDs is applied with (Gd,Y)3(Ga,Al)5O12: Ce3+ yellow phosphor on a commercial UV-LED chip, achieving strong cold white emission. Our finding provides a new idea to enhance blue PL QYs of Na+-doped CDs for their further potential applications in optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sodium-doped carbon dots
Silver nitrate
Photoluminescence quantum yields
Light emitting diode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of formation of the Na + -doped CDs. The bottom right of figure shows the HR-TEM image of Na+-doped CDs.

(a) The PL spectra of Na+-doped CDs synthesized at different molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor. The inset shows the photography of S-5 illuminated by ultraviolet light at 325 nm. (b) The integrated PL intensity of the sample versus the molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of S - 0, S - 1, S - 5, S - 25. (b) The excitation spectra of S - 0, S - 1, S - 5, S - 25 at an emission wavelength of 310 nm.
The emission spectra of (a) S-0, (b) S-1, (c), and S-5 (d) S-25 at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Room-temperature PL decay traces recorded at 436 nm from the sample S - 0, S - 1, S −5 and S - 25. (b) The fitting results of PL decays measured for the samples.

(a) Normalized FTIR absorption spectra of the Na+-doped CDs synthesized at different molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor. (b) FTIR absorption spectrum of the sample S-1. The dashed line corresponds to deconvolution of FTIR signals from N–H and O–H bonds. The inset displays the intensity of the deconvoluted bands versus the molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor.

(a) PL spectrum of the blue emitting Na+-doped CDs with (Gd,Y)3(Ga,Al)5O12: Ce3+ yellow phosphor on the UV-LED chip with 365 nm emission. The inset shows the photograph of the fabricated white LED operated at a current of 200 mA. (b) Operational stability of the corresponding white LED operated at a current of 200 mA. The inset shows the emission spectra of white LED measured under continuous UV light illumination.

head.ce:title: Influence of silver nitrate in obtaining highly luminescent sodium-doped carbon dots for white light emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Nanophotonic Functional Materials and Devices, School of Information and Optoelectronic Science and Engineering, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Nanophotonic Functional Materials and Devices
School of Information and Optoelectronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Nanophotonic Functional Materials and Devices, School of Information and Optoelectronic Science and Engineering, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Hanshan Normal University, Chaozhou 521041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, Hanshan Normal University, Chaozhou 521041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent metal-doping carbon dots (CDs) possess excellent optical properties for light-converting phosphors of white light emitting diode (LED). However, the photoluminescence quantum yields (PL QYs) of CDs still suffer from the trapping defect at the surface of CDs which may generate the detrimental nonradiative recombination centers. Herein, we developed an N doping method to enhance blue light emission of Na+-doped CDs via a modified solid-state reaction, using silver nitrate as nitrogen source. The blue PL intensity form Na+-doped CDs is improved by the increasing molar ratio of silver nitrate precursor. The obtained Na+ and N co-doping CDs exhibited 5-fold enhancement of PL intensity with a PL QYs up to 24% as compared to their undoped counterpart. Based on the temporal evolution of PL and surface structure characteristics, it suggested that the improvement of PL QYs derived from the elimination of the trapping defects at the surface of Na+-doped CDs. This is realized by the introduction of nitrate groups and Ag+ ions, which can reduce the formation of hydroxyl radicals related nonradiative recombination centers. Furthermore, the Na+ and N co-doped CDs is applied with (Gd,Y)3(Ga,Al)5O12: Ce3+ yellow phosphor on a commercial UV-LED chip, achieving strong cold white emission. Our finding provides a new idea to enhance blue PL QYs of Na+-doped CDs for their further potential applications in optoelectronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the generation of efficient white light emitting diode (LED) is fabricated by combination of a 450 nm LED chip with the yellow-emitting phosphor due to the high efficiency of 450 nm LED chip [1]. Considering that such blue rich white LED often suffer from a poor color rendering index (CRI) and light toxicity to an eye, to design a white LED based on UV 365 nm LED chip arising from minimizing blue light would be favorable for the development of healthy white LED with high CRI. Benefiting from strong deep-UV absorption (range from 360 to 400 nm) and high photoluminescence quantum yields (PL QYs) of carbon nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials have recently emerged as one of the promising phosphors for white LED based on UV 365 nm LED chip [2–4]. In fact, carbon nanomaterials, as new emerging luminescent nanomaterials and advanced energy conversion materials, have attracted much attentions due to its widely practical applications in energy storage, optoelectronic devices and biosensor [5–10]. In the recent years, sodium (Na) ion batteries with carbon anodes have been considered as a promising alternative to lithium (Li) ion batteries owing to the abundance of Na resources and their low cost [11–13]. Sun et al. reported that a high reversible electrode capacity up to 372.4 mA g h−1 for the hard carbon anodes based Na ion batteries by using graphene as a multi-functional binder [14]. It is theoretically predicted that the carbon anode based Na ion batteries has an ultra-high theoretical capacity of 1166 mA h g−1 and a low negative electro-chemical potential of 2.714 V [15]. However, past research in Na+-doped carbon nanomaterials has primarily focused on the strategies for improving the capacity and high-rate performance of Na ion batteries [16–18]. So far, few studies have been conducted on the internal photophysical properties and their advanced optoelectronic applications of Na+-doped carbon dots (CDs). In fact, it has been demonstrated that heteroatom doping or the surface configuration are more effective at tuning the emission color and enhancing the photoluminescence quantum yields (PL QYs) of CDs [19–21]. Among these, metal ions-doping can more efficiently modulate the band structure and enhance PL QYs of CDs than the nonmetallic heteroatoms due to their donor nature [22,23]. Furthermore, the effects of metal chelator are beneficial to passivate the electronegative oxygen related defects in CDs. For example, Jayaweera et al. synthesized the blue luminescent CDs with the PL QYs of 28.7% though the Al-doping [24]. Xu et al. reported the luminescent Zn-doping CDs exhibited PL QYs up to 32.3% using hydrothermal method [25]. It is worth to mention that by Na+ cations into CDs, the electronic surface states of CDs can be effectively manipulated, leading to the enchantment of PL QYs and multifarious light emission [26–28]. Particularly, efficient emission from Na+-doped CDs has been discovered [26–28], but the promising method to improve the optical properties of CDs are still in its infancy.

Previously, we have demonstrated that the enhanced blue PL QYs and reduced PL bandwidth from the CDs via Na+ doping [29]. Unfortunately, as the Na+ concentration was further increased by ∼3 times than the original (SI-Table 1), it is found that there is no obvious enhancement in PL intensity continuously, as shown in Fig. S1. Usually, the nitrogen (N) doping is a common strategy to improve the PL QYs of CDs because N has similar atomic size and electron structure compared to C [30–32]. It has demonstrated that the metal ions and nitrogen (N) co-doping can efficiently improve the PL QYs of CDs due to the form of metal-N coordinating functional knots [33]. Aspired by our previous success, in the present work, we developed luminescent Na+ and N co-doped CDs via a modified solid-state reaction, using silver nitrate as nitrogen source. It is found that the participation of silver nitrate (AgNO3) precursor can significantly enhance blue light emission of Na+-doped CDs. More importantly, the resulted Na+ and N co-doped CDs exhibit efficient blue light emission with the PL QYs of 24%. The enhanced blue PL mechanism was extensively studied though the evolution of PL characteristics and surface structure characterization. Furthermore, the Na+ and N co-doped CDs is applied with (Gd,Y)3(Ga,Al)5O12: Ce3+ yellow phosphor on a commercial UV light emitting diode (LED) chip, achieving strong cold white emission.

The Na+-doped CDs were fabricated via a modified solid-state reaction method with the different molar ratio of sodium hydroxide, trisodium citrate and silver nitrate, as shown in Fig. 1 . The molar ratio of sodium hydroxide, trisodium citrate were kept at 1 : 1, while the molar ratio of silver nitrate were varied from 0 to 0.05. Samples were named by S-n (n = 0, 1, 5, 25) for the molar ratio of silver nitrate at 0, 0.002, 0.01, and 0.05, respectively. Firstly, 5 mL 0.1 M sodium hydroxide ethanol solution and 0.1 M trisodium citrate powder was mixed and put it into the mortar for grinding. Then 0.01 M/mL silver nitrate ethanol solution with different volume ratio were added in the mortar and the whole mixture was kept stirring. Once the thick-viscous and brown mixture were obtained, they were transferred to a vacuum oven and dried at 60 °C until ethanol is completely evaporated. And then the temperature was ramped to 180 °C for heating 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the crude CDs products were obtained. In order to filter out the impurities in the CDs, the products were dialyzed against deionized water for 48 h using a 1000Da dialysis bag. Finally, the obtained purified Na+-doped CDs were dispersed in the deionized water with the concentration of 10 mg/mL. The PL characteristics of the Na+-doped CDs were measured by using a PL spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instrument). The UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV–Vis 3600 spectrophotometer. The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were measured on FEI Talos F200X. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) absorption spectra were carried out by using a Shimadzu IRAffinity-1S spectrometer.

The morphology and structure of the Na+-doped CDs was characterized by HR-TEM, as shown in the bottom right of Fig. 1. The TEM image reveals that the Na+-doped CDs have a mean particle size of ∼5 nm as well as good dispersity in aqueous solution. Furthermore, it shows that the spacing lattice fringes of the Na+-doped CDs is 0.22 nm corresponding to the <100> crystalline direction of graphite. Fig. 2 (a) presents the PL spectra of Na+-doped CDs dispersed in aqueous solution with different molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor under an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. It can be seen that all the PL spectra of the Na + -doped CDs exhibited blue light emission peaking at ∼436 nm with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of ∼65 nm. The PL intensity of Na + -doped CDs aqueous solution tended to increase with increasing the molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor. The highest PL emission intensity was observed as increasing the molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor up to 0.01 M, as shown in Fig. 2 (b). Interestingly, the Na+-doped CDs (S-5) with a PLQY of 23.32% were obtained, as shown in Fig. S2. The PL intensity significantly increases by more than ∼5 times as compared with the sample S-0. It is interesting to find that the very bright blue light emission from the sample S-5 can be perceptible to the naked eye in a bright room when illuminated by ultraviolet light at 325 nm. However, with further increasing the molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor up to 0.05 M, the blue emission intensity of Na+-doped CDs decreases rapidly. This phenomenon indicate that the AgNO3 plays an important role in the enhancing PL intensity of Na + -doped CDs.

The absorption spectra and PL excitation (PLE) spectra of the samples were employed to analyze the origin of PL characteristics. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), an absorption shoulder S at ∼313 nm can be observed from the UV–vis absorption spectra of all the samples, which is attributed to the n - π* transition of the C–N or/and CO [34]. From Fig. 3 (b), the PLE spectra of all samples exhibit an intense broad band peaked at around 313 nm. Comparing the UV–vis absorption spectra with PLE spectra, it can be asserted that the blue light emission from the Na+-doped CDs is strongly correlated with the C–N or CO functional group. In order to further understand the origin of blue PL emission, the PL spectra of all the samples were excited at different excitation wavelength, as displayed in Fig. 4 . The PL spectra shape and dominant PL peak position for all samples is independent of the excitation wavelength ranging from 250 nm to 350 nm. This PL behavior is completely different from the band tail recombination model in which the characteristics of PL peak position and full width at half-maximum dependence on the excitation wavelength. Therefore, it is reasonable to consider that the blue light emission from the Na+-doped CDs is originated from the defect state transitions [35,36].

For gaining a better understand the nature of the enhancing blue light emission in the Na+-doped CDs, the PL decay spectra were carried out, as shown in Fig. 5 . The decay traces can be well fitted to double exponential decay function:
I
( t )
=
I 0
+ A
1
exp
(
− t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
(
− t
τ 2
)
where I0 is the background level, τ1 and τ2 gives the fast decay lifetime and the slow decay lifetime with their amplitudes (weighting factors) A1 and A2, respectively [35]. The fast decay component τ1 can be correlated with carrier decay time influenced by non-radiative defects while the slow decay component τ2 is correlated with the radiative recombination process [37]. The fitting results of time-resolved PL decay traces for the Na+-doped CDs synthesized at different molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor were also listed in Fig. 5 (b). The weighted average PL lifetime is given by (A1 *τ1 2 + A2 *τ2 2)/(A1 *τ1 + A2 *τ2) [38]. It can be seen that all the Na+-doped CDs synthesized at different molar ratio of AgNO3 exhibit an ultrafast ns PL decay with the lifetime of ∼5.7 ns, which is consistent with that observed in pure Na+-doped CDs in previous work [29]. According to our previous work, this blue light emission is ascribed to luminescent Na–O related defect centers in the band gap. In fact, it is noteworthy that with increasing the molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor, the weight ratio of slow decay component τ2 gradually increase. This indicates that the participation of AgNO3 precursor in the synthesis process of Na + -doped CDs can effectively remove the nonradiative recombination defect, resulted in the behavior of enhancing PL intensity.

The FTIR absorption spectra were performed to trace the chemical bonding configurations of the Na+-doped CDs synthesized at different molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). For the sample S-0, the FTIR absorption spectrum mainly displayed the following absorption bands: the two intense absorption bands at 1403 and 1589 cm−1 is associated with the C–O and CO stretching vibrations of sodium carboxyl groups, respectively [39]. The absorption bands located at 649 cm−1 comes from the stretching vibration of C–H bonds [28]. In addition, the 3450 cm−1 broad band is corresponding to the O–H stretching vibrations [40]. Upon adding 0.002 M of AgNO3 precursor in the reaction solution, a weak absorbing band appear at 2352 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of carbonyl groups [41]. Particularly, from Fig. 6 (b), it is found that there is a broad absorption band centered at 3250 cm−1 for sample S-1, which is associated with N–H stretching vibrations [42–44]. The intensity for the deconvoluted absorption bands of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations as a function is shown in the inset of Fig. 6 (b). One can see that the intensity of N–H bonds gradually increases with increasing the molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor. In contrast, the intensity of the O–H bonds stretching mode rapidly dropped. This may be due to an increase in the nitrate ion concentration resulted in a higher degree of surface oxidation. Apparently, the increasing molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor resulted in the chemical bonds reconstruction of Na + -doped CDs. In fact, it is well known that the PL characteristics of CDs is sensitive to the surface functionalization. Xu et al. reported that the hydroxyl radicals in the CDs could quench the visible emission intensity of CDs due to their electron accepting nature [25]. In our case, the introduction of AgNO3 precursor induces the nitrate groups into the structure of CDs and then the nitrate groups will replace the hydroxyl radicals at the surface of CDs to reduce the surface trap-assisted non-radiative recombination. Meanwhile, the negatively charged hydroxyl radicals on the surface of CDs also can be removed by Ag+ ions, which can be reduced to elemental Ag. This was also confirmed by the increase of the weight ratio of slow decay component τ2 with increasing the molar ratio of AgNO3. However, the excessive Ag+ ions has a negative effect on the PL of Na+-doped CDs by in situ growth of the Ag nanoparticles on the surface, which can be excellent sensor for Ag+ detection [45]. Thus, the Na+-doped CDs shows the lower PL intensity as further increasing the molar ratio of AgNO3 precursor.

Finally, we placed the blue emitting Na+-doped CDs with (Gd,Y)3(Ga,Al)5O12: Ce3+ yellow phosphor as color conversion materials on a commercial UV-LED chip with 365 nm emission. The (Gd,Y)3(Ga,Al)5O12: Ce3+ yellow phosphor is composited with Na + -doped CDs with a ratio of 1 : 5. As shown in Fig. S3, the luminance of LED increases gradually with increasing the injected current and reach to ∼4520 cd m2 at an injected current of 200 mA. Fig. 7 shows the luminous characteristics of the fabricated LED under 200 mA current. From Fig. 7 (a), it can be seen that the fabricated LED shows the strong white light emission with a CCT of 5322 K and CIE coordinates of (0.33, 0.24). Moreover, the photostability of the LED was further evaluated as shown in Fig. 7 (b). It is shown that the luminance of obtained LED remained stable operation for 300 min in air. The present results demonstrate that Na+-doped CDs show a potential candidate for commercial white LED applications.

In summary, we developed an N doping method to enhance blue light emission of Na+-doped CDs via a modified solid-state reaction, using silver nitrate as nitrogen source. The PL intensity of the Na+ and N co-doping CDs significantly increases by more than ∼5 times as compared with those of their undoped counterpart. The obtained Na+ and N co-doping CDs exhibited a PL QYs up to 24%. The effects of the participation of AgNO3 precursor on enhancing blue light emission of Na + -doped CDs were studied in detail. Based on the evolution of PL and surface structure characteristics, it suggested that the introduction of nitrate groups replace the hydroxyl radicals at the surface of CDs resulted in reducing the surface trap-assisted non-radiative recombination. Furthermore, the white LED is demonstrated by combining the Na+-doped CDs and (Gd,Y)3(Ga,Al)5O12: Ce3+ yellow phosphor. Our finding provides a new idea to improve the performance of Na + -doped CDs for their further potential applications in optoelectronic devices.

Jiaxin Yang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis; Qiwen Zeng: Investigation, Formal analysis; Jie Song: Investigation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing; Shaolong Tie: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation; Zhenxu Lin: Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 11874020and 12174123), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Reuter Foundation (2020A1515010432) and Program of Hanshan Normal University (XN201918).
